section.title: Against Egypt
section.sectionRationale: Egypt, Judah's tempting but unreliable ally against Babylon, is judged here for the same root sin as Tyre: proud self sufficiency, pictured as Pharaoh's boast that the Nile, and Egypt's whole existence, belongs to himself alone. The chapter promises Egypt's desolation, a limited exile and partial restoration, and closes by paying Babylon with Egypt's spoils as wages for her long labor against Tyre.
section.thesis: Egypt's pride in self sufficient power, and Judah's foolish trust in that broken reed of an ally, both fall before the LORD who alone raises up and humbles nations, though even in judgment He measures Egypt's exile and leaves room for a modest, humbled future.
section.frameNote: Three movements: Pharaoh's boast that the Nile is his own answered by God's judgment and Egypt's failure as Israel's ally; the promise of forty years of desolation and scattering followed by a limited restoration to a lowly kingdom; and Egypt's wealth given to Nebuchadnezzar as wages for his long, unpaid labor against Tyre.

units.label: Pharaoh's boast answered
units.note: the Nile is mine, I made it

verses.text: In the tenth year, on the twelfth day of the tenth month, the word of the LORD came to me, saying,
“Son of man, set your face against Pharaoh king of Egypt and prophesy against him and against all Egypt.

Speak to him and tell him that this is what the Lord GOD says: Behold, I am against you, O Pharaoh king of Egypt, O great {{tannin|monster}} who lies among his rivers, who says, 'The Nile is mine; I made it myself.'
But I will put hooks in your jaws and cause the fish of your streams to cling to your scales. I will haul you up out of your rivers, and all the fish of your streams will cling to your scales.

I will leave you in the desert, you and all the fish of your streams. You will fall on the open field and will not be taken away or gathered for burial. I have given you as food to the beasts of the earth and the birds of the air.
Then all the people of Egypt will know that I am the LORD. For you were only a staff of reeds to the house of Israel.
When Israel took hold of you with their hands, you splintered, tearing all their shoulders; when they leaned on you, you broke, and their backs were wrenched.
Therefore this is what the Lord GOD says: I will bring a sword against you and cut off from you man and beast.
The land of Egypt will become a desolate wasteland. Then they will know that I am the LORD. Because you said, 'The Nile is mine; I made it,'
therefore I am against you and against your rivers. I will turn the land of Egypt into a ruin, a desolate wasteland from Migdol to Syene, and as far as the border of Cush.
No foot of man or beast will pass through, and it will be uninhabited for forty years.

units.label: Forty years, then a lowly kingdom

verses.text: I will make the land of Egypt a desolation among desolate lands, and her cities will lie desolate for forty years among the ruined cities. And I will disperse the Egyptians among the nations and scatter them throughout the countries.
For this is what the Lord GOD says: At the end of forty years I will gather the Egyptians from the nations to which they were scattered.
I will restore Egypt from captivity and bring them back to the land of Pathros, the land of their origin. There they will be a lowly kingdom.
Egypt will be the lowliest of kingdoms and will never again exalt itself above the nations. For I will diminish Egypt so that it will never again rule over the nations.
Egypt will never again be an object of trust for the house of Israel, but will remind them of their iniquity in turning to the Egyptians. Then they will know that I am the Lord GOD.”
In the twenty-seventh year, on the first day of the first month, the word of the LORD came to me, saying,

“Son of man, Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon caused his army to labor strenuously against Tyre. Every head was made bald and every shoulder made raw. But he and his army received no wages from Tyre for the labor they expended on it.

Therefore this is what the Lord GOD says: I will give the land of Egypt to Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, who will carry off its wealth, seize its spoil, and remove its plunder. This will be the wages for his army.
I have given him the land of Egypt as the reward for his labor, because it was done for Me, declares the Lord GOD.
In that day I will cause a {{qeren|horn}} to sprout for the house of Israel, and I will open your mouth to speak among them. Then they will know that I am the LORD.”

tannin.gloss: A great sea or river monster, sometimes a crocodile, elsewhere used of the primeval chaos monster subdued by God at creation. Pharaoh, who claims to have made the Nile himself, is cast as this very monster, a creature God alone made and can just as easily haul out and destroy.

qeren.gloss: A horn, a common biblical image for strength, dignity, or a raised, victorious power, often associated with kings or deliverers. The promise that a horn will sprout for Israel points to a future restoration of strength precisely when Egypt, her former source of misplaced trust, is most thoroughly humbled.

basic.context: This chapter opens the Egypt sequence, chapters 29 to 32, with Pharaoh judged for the same root sin already condemned in Tyre, proud self sufficiency, here expressed in his boast that he made the Nile himself. The chapter also names Egypt's specific failure as an ally, a staff of reeds that splintered and wounded Israel's hand rather than supporting it, and closes with a striking historical note: Egypt's wealth given to Nebuchadnezzar as overdue wages for his long, costly siege of Tyre.
basic.hermeneutics: This describes real, dated historical judgments: Egypt's devastation under Babylonian pressure, a genuine period of decline and diminished influence following this era, and a specific historical transaction in which Egypt's resources compensated Babylon for the Tyre campaign. The forty years of desolation and Egypt's promised lowly restoration describe an actual historical trajectory of decline for Egypt's regional power, not merely a symbolic idea, even where the precise chronology of 'forty years' is debated among historians.
basic.originalLanguages: Pharaoh, who claims 'I made the Nile myself,' is called a tannin, a great river monster, the very kind of primeval chaos creature Scripture elsewhere says only God can subdue, an ironic reversal of his boast. And the chapter closes with a promise that a qeren, a horn, a picture of restored strength and dignity, will sprout for Israel exactly when her former, misplaced source of trust lies humbled.
basic.typology: Egypt cast down as a defeated sea monster, later restored only as 'the lowliest of kingdoms,' anticipates the wider biblical pattern of every proud human power being subdued, while the horn promised to Israel points forward to the ultimate horn of salvation raised up in the house of David, Luke 1:69, Christ Himself.
basic.christ: Egypt's proud self sufficiency, 'the Nile is mine, I made it,' is answered by the God who alone made the Nile and everything in it; where human empires boast of self made power, Christ alone is the true horn of salvation, raised not from human strength but from God's own faithfulness to His covenant people, Luke 1:68-69.
basic.god: God is sovereign over the nations' rise and fall, using even one pagan empire, Babylon, to discipline another, Egypt, and paying Babylon's very real, uncompensated military labor with Egypt's own wealth, showing His justice attends to detail even in international affairs between nations who do not know Him.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 19 and Jeremiah 46 give companion oracles against Egypt. 2 Kings 18:21 and Isaiah 36:6 both use the image of Egypt as a splintering reed of a staff, a broken support for anyone who leans on her, closely matching this chapter's verse 6 to 7. Luke 1:68-69 speaks of God raising up 'a horn of salvation' in the house of David, echoing this chapter's closing promise for Israel.
basic.oneStory: Egypt's proud self sufficiency and Israel's foolish trust in her as an ally both fall before the LORD, who alone sustains and topples nations; yet even here, in the very chapter that humbles Egypt to her lowest point, God promises a horn of restored strength for Israel, a promise finally and fully answered in the true horn of salvation, Jesus Christ, raised up in the house of David.

context.literary: Opens the four chapter Egypt sequence, 29 to 32, the longest sustained oracle against any single nation in this collection, following the same broad pattern already seen with Tyre: judgment on pride, a period of desolation, and a measured hope of limited restoration, framed here by two distinct dated oracles, verses 1 to 16 and 17 to 21.
context.historical: Egypt under the Twenty Sixth Dynasty repeatedly encouraged Judah's kings to rebel against Babylon while failing to provide real military support, most famously abandoning Jerusalem to Babylonian siege in Jeremiah's day; Nebuchadnezzar's later campaign against Egypt, following his costly and only partially compensated siege of Tyre, is independently corroborated by fragmentary Babylonian records referencing an invasion of Egypt in 568 to 567 BC.
context.canonical: Opens the Egypt oracles that close out the collection of nation oracles, chapters 25 to 32, and stands alongside Isaiah 19 to 20 and Jeremiah 46 as the major prophetic treatments of Egypt's judgment and, uniquely in Isaiah's case, her eventual place among the LORD's worshiping peoples.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To declare that Egypt's pride in her own self sufficient power, and Israel's foolish reliance on her as a military ally, both stand under the LORD's judgment, while still promising Egypt a measured, humbled future and Israel a renewed horn of strength once her misplaced trust is stripped away.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage describes a specific historical judgment on Egypt for specific sins, self deifying pride and unreliable alliance, and a specific historical transaction paying Babylon with Egypt's wealth; it does not prescribe a general foreign policy. Its abiding principle is that trusting any human power in place of God, and any nation's pride in self sufficiency, invites correction.

debates.question: Was the 'forty years' of Egypt's desolation, verses 11 to 13, and the payment of Egypt to Nebuchadnezzar, verses 17 to 20, fulfilled with strict historical literalness?

views.label: A literal forty year period and a specific, historically attested Babylonian campaign
views.case: Nebuchadnezzar's invasion of Egypt is referenced in a fragmentary Babylonian chronicle dated to around 568 to 567 BC, and ancient Egyptian sources describe genuine periods of internal weakness and foreign domination in the following decades, supporting a real, roughly forty year window of diminished Egyptian power consistent with a straightforward reading.

views.label: A round, symbolic number describing a complete but not necessarily exact period of humbling
views.case: 'Forty years' recurs throughout Scripture, the wilderness wandering, various periods of oppression in Judges, as an idiomatic figure for a complete generation or a full measure of testing rather than a strictly literal count of days, so the emphasis falls on the totality of Egypt's humbling rather than a precise calendar span.

debates.bearing: Either reading affirms the same historical core, that Egypt did suffer a real, extended period of weakened power and foreign domination following this era, and that Babylon's Egyptian campaign, however its dating is reckoned, is independently corroborated outside Scripture, supporting the prophecy's substantial historical fulfillment.

study.originalLanguages: Pharaoh, boasting 'I made the Nile myself,' is named a tannin, the great river monster elsewhere associated with the primeval chaos only God can subdue, exposing his claim as absurd overreach. And in the very chapter that humbles Egypt most fully, God promises Israel a qeren, a horn, a picture of restored dignity and strength precisely when her old, misplaced source of confidence lies broken.

typology.type: Egypt cast down as a defeated sea monster, later restored only as 'the lowliest of kingdoms'
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates every proud human power being subdued before God, while the horn promised to Israel in the same chapter points forward to the true horn of salvation raised up in the house of David, Luke 1:69, fulfilled in Christ.

study.christ: Egypt's proud self sufficiency, 'the Nile is mine, I made it myself,' is answered by the God who alone truly made the Nile and everything living in it. Where human empires boast of self made greatness, the horn of salvation promised to Israel in this very chapter finds its true and lasting fulfillment not in any earthly power but in Christ, raised up from the house of David by God's own faithfulness rather than human strength, Luke 1:68-69.
study.god: God is sovereign over the rise and fall of nations, using one pagan empire, Babylon, to discipline another, Egypt, and even settling Babylon's very real, uncompensated military debt from the Tyre campaign with Egypt's own wealth. His justice reaches into the ordinary transactions between nations who do not yet know Him, showing that nothing lies outside His sovereign accounting.

crossReferences.note: A companion oracle against Egypt that, uniquely, ends with Egypt's eventual worship of the LORD alongside Israel and Assyria.
Egypt is described as a splintering reed of a staff for anyone who leans on her, closely paralleling this chapter's imagery in verses 6 to 7.
A parallel oracle against Egypt from roughly the same historical period, sharing many of the same themes of pride and coming judgment.
Zechariah's song celebrates God raising up 'a horn of salvation' in the house of David, echoing this chapter's closing promise for Israel and finding its fulfillment in Christ.

study.oneStory: Egypt's proud self sufficiency and Israel's foolish trust in her as an ally both fall before the LORD, who alone sustains and topples nations. Yet even in the very chapter that humbles Egypt to her lowest point, God promises a horn of restored strength for His people, a promise finally and fully answered in the true horn of salvation, Jesus Christ, raised up in the house of David by grace rather than by human power.

quiz.q: What specific boast provoked judgment against Pharaoh in this chapter?

options.text: He claimed, 'The Nile is mine; I made it myself.'
options.note: Verse 3, repeated in verse 9, gives this exact claim as the provoking sin.

options.text: He refused to send tribute to Babylon.
options.note: Not mentioned as the specific accusation here; the sin named is his boast of self made power over the Nile.

options.text: He built new temples to foreign gods.
options.note: Not described in this text; the accusation concerns his claim over the Nile itself, not temple building.

options.text: He conquered territory that belonged to Israel.
options.note: Not the stated sin; the specific boast condemned is his self sufficient claim over the Nile's origin.

quiz.q: How does the chapter describe Egypt's failure as an ally to Israel?

options.text: As a staff of reeds that splintered and wrenched the backs of those who leaned on it.
options.note: Verses 6 to 7 use exactly this image of a broken, wounding support.

options.text: As a faithful ally who kept every promise of military aid.
options.note: The opposite is described; Egypt is condemned precisely for being an unreliable, harmful support.

options.text: As a nation that never had any relationship with Israel.
options.note: The text assumes a real history of Israel relying on Egypt, which then failed them.

options.text: As a wealthy trading partner with no military role at all.
options.note: The specific image used, a staff people leaned on, points to a military and political support role, not merely trade.

quiz.q: What would happen to Egypt for forty years, according to verses 11 to 13?

options.text: It would be uninhabited and desolate, with its people dispersed among the nations.
options.note: Verses 11 to 12 explicitly describe this forty year desolation and scattering.

options.text: It would enjoy forty years of unprecedented prosperity.
options.note: The opposite is described; the forty years is a period of desolation and scattering, not prosperity.

options.text: It would be ruled directly by Israelite governors.
options.note: Not described in this text; the passage speaks of desolation and later a return of Egypt's own scattered people.

options.text: It would be entirely resettled by Babylonian colonists permanently.
options.note: The text promises the Egyptians themselves would later be gathered back, not permanent replacement by Babylonian settlers.

quiz.q: Why, according to verses 17 to 20, would Nebuchadnezzar be given the land of Egypt?

options.text: As wages for his army's long, costly, and previously uncompensated labor against Tyre.
options.note: Verses 18 to 20 state this directly, tying Egypt's wealth to unpaid Babylonian labor at Tyre.

options.text: As punishment for Nebuchadnezzar's own personal pride.
options.note: The text presents this as a reward, not a punishment, for Nebuchadnezzar.

options.text: Because Egypt had never sinned and this was an unrelated political transaction.
options.note: Egypt's own sin is the subject of the earlier oracle in the same chapter; this is not presented as unrelated to her judgment.

options.text: Because Egypt requested Babylonian protection voluntarily.
options.note: The text frames this as the LORD's sovereign decision to compensate Babylon, not a request initiated by Egypt.

quiz.q: What promise does the chapter give Israel in its closing verse, 21?

options.text: That a horn will sprout for the house of Israel, and Ezekiel's mouth will be opened among them.
options.note: Verse 21 states both of these promises explicitly at the chapter's close.

options.text: That Israel would immediately conquer Egypt themselves.
options.note: No such conquest is promised; the promise concerns a horn of restored strength and Ezekiel's opened mouth.

options.text: That Israel would be permanently exempt from all future judgment.
options.note: Not stated in this verse; the promise is specifically about a horn sprouting and the prophet's mouth being opened.

options.text: That Egypt would become Israel's new capital city.
options.note: Nothing in the text suggests this; the promise concerns Israel's own restored strength, not Egyptian territory becoming Israel's capital.

questions: Pharaoh boasted, 'The Nile is mine; I made it myself,' crediting himself for what only God had given. What resource, ability, or blessing in your life do you quietly credit to yourself rather than to God's provision?
Egypt is pictured as a staff of reeds that splintered and wounded anyone who leaned on it. What have you leaned on for security that, in the end, only let you down or hurt you?

God's justice reached even into the fine details of an international transaction between two pagan empires, paying Babylon with Egypt's wealth. Where do you need to trust that God sees and will settle accounts in situations that feel unfair or unresolved in your own life?

The chapter promises that Egypt would never again be an object of trust for Israel, so they would remember their sin in turning to her. What past mistake or misplaced trust does God want you to remember, not with shame, but as a safeguard against repeating it?

The horn of salvation promised to Israel here finds its fulfillment in Christ, raised up by God's grace rather than human strength. Where in your life right now do you need to remember that your hope rests on God's faithfulness, not on your own or anyone else's self sufficient power?

translationNotes.note: The Hebrew tannin, translated 'monster' here, can also mean a large crocodile or serpent, and elsewhere in Scripture describes the primeval chaos creature only God can subdue, Job 41; either sense fits Pharaoh's boastful, godlike self image being exposed as false.

Pathros refers to Upper, or southern, Egypt, considered by ancient Egyptians themselves to be the land of their origin, making the promised return there a deliberate return to Egypt's own ancestral homeland rather than a foreign resettlement.

This verse and the wider oracle in verses 17 to 21 are dated to the twenty seventh year of Ezekiel's exile, making it the latest dated oracle in the entire book, delivered years after the fall of Jerusalem and even after Nebuchadnezzar's long siege of Tyre had concluded.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture tool for Ezekiel 29:1-21. No verified sermon from the trusted cluster directly expounding this specific passage was found this session; sermon status is none.
